items.correct: The Earth spinning on its axis roughly once a day.
items.misconception: The Sun moving around the Earth.
items.why: Rotation turns each place toward and away from the Sun, giving day and night.

items.correct: The tilt of the Earth's axis as it orbits the Sun.
items.misconception: The Earth being closer to the Sun in summer.
items.random_wrong: The Sun getting hotter in summer.
items.why: Axial tilt changes how directly sunlight strikes each hemisphere, not the distance to the Sun.

items.q: In which direction does the Sun appear to rise?
items.why: Because the Earth rotates west-to-east, the Sun appears to rise in the east and set in the west.

items.q: The Sun appears to rise in the:
items.why: It's East. “North” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: West to east. “East to west” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many hours in one full rotation?
items.why: The real figure is 24. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
